title: SEGMENT 18 — Where this leads — the next generation of N-of-1 therapies, splice-switching pipeline, the limits of personalization

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — Where this leads — the next generation of N-of-1 therapies, splice-switching pipeline, the limits of personalization
Let me close with a look forward, because the milasen case has implications well beyond the one patient it was made for.
The first and most concrete extension of the milasen approach is the growing pipeline of splice-switching ASOs for other rare genetic diseases.

Whenever a patient is found whose disease is caused by a splicing mutation — a cryptic splice site activated by an intronic insertion, a pseudoexon inserted by an aberrant splice event, an exon-skipping defect — the milasen template can in principle be applied.
Design an ASO against the splice site that needs to be masked.
Validate in patient cells.
Run an abbreviated preclinical program.
Submit to FDA under Expanded Access.
Dose under careful monitoring.
Each one of those steps was charted by the milasen case, and each one becomes faster and easier as the field accumulates experience.
A growing number of such cases have been published since 2019.
Patients with rare forms of various neurological diseases, retinal diseases, and metabolic diseases have been treated with custom-designed splice-modulating ASOs.

Most of these cases have not had as much publicity as milasen, but collectively they represent a steady accumulation of evidence that the platform is robust, the regulatory pathway is workable, and the clinical outcomes — variable, modest, sometimes meaningful, never curative on their own — are real.
Beyond splice-switching ASOs, the broader concept of N-of-1 personalized therapy has begun to expand into other molecular modalities.
RNA editing approaches that can correct specific base changes are being adapted for individual patients.
CRISPR-based genome editing is being considered for individualized in-vivo applications.
Antibody-drug conjugates tailored to specific patient mutations are being explored.

The general principle that the milasen case crystallized — that for a small subset of patients with rare diseases caused by molecularly tractable mutations, a fully custom drug can be feasibly designed, made, and delivered — is now being explored across multiple drug classes, not just ASOs.
The bottleneck, increasingly, is not the technology.
The chemistry of ASOs is mature.
The bioinformatics for identifying tractable mutations are mature.
The regulatory pathway, while still case-by-case, has been blazed.

The bottleneck is the workflow — the ability to identify the right patients, the funding to support the custom development, the institutional capacity to deliver and monitor the therapies, and the ethical and economic structures to decide which patients to prioritize when the available capacity is, inevitably, less than the demand.
I want to close with what I think the honest limits of personalization are, because the milasen story can be read in two opposite ways and I want you to be able to hold both readings at once.
The optimistic reading is that we have crossed a threshold.
We are no longer limited, for rare disease patients, to whatever drugs the pharmaceutical industry has happened to develop.
We can, when the molecular pathology is right, design a custom drug for a specific patient, and we can do it in months.
This is a real and meaningful expansion of what medicine can offer.
For families who would previously have had no option, the milasen story is a promise that an option may now exist.
The sobering reading is that "may now exist" is doing a lot of work in that sentence.
The patients who benefit from this approach are a small subset of all rare disease patients.
They are the ones whose mutations are amenable to ASO therapy or some other tractable modality.
They are the ones whose disease is at a stage where slowing progression is still meaningful.
They are the ones whose families have the resources and the connections to engage with the academic medical centers and non-profit foundations that can deliver this kind of care.
And even for those who do qualify and do receive treatment, the results are usually not cures.
They are clinical improvements.
Sometimes substantial.
Often partial.
Rarely complete.
The honest answer to the question I asked at the beginning of this lecture — can we build a medicine for one person?
— is yes, sometimes, for some patients, with limited expectations.
That is a much smaller answer than "yes, period," but it is much larger than the answer the field would have given before milasen.
And it is, I think, the answer we should hold steadily as the field continues to grow.
Mila Makovec lived ten years.

The drug that was made for her did not save her life, but it improved her last years measurably, and the case that her treatment created has now become the template for an emerging field that is, today, designing and delivering custom drugs for patients who, a decade ago, would have had nothing offered to them at all.
That is, in a way, what one patient's life can do for the patients who come after her.
The Mila's Miracle Foundation.
The n-Lorem Foundation.
The Oversight Committee on Personalized Experimental Therapeutics.
The published paper itself.
The trained generation of researchers and clinicians and regulators who learned, from this case, that an individualized drug development workflow is possible.
All of those are, in a sense, things she left behind.
The drug bore her name.
The platform she made possible is now bearing other patients' names too.
If you take one thing from this lecture, take that.
The question of whether we can build a medicine for one person has been answered.

The much harder question — how to make sure every patient who could benefit actually does, and how to keep our promises honest about what those medicines can and cannot do — is the question the next generation of this field, your generation if you go into medicine, will spend the next several decades answering.
Thank you for your attention today.
We'll take questions after the break.
